_comment: Source of truth for changelog.html's 'Recent updates' list. Regenerated by tools/gen_changelog.mjs from merged PRs carrying both the 'changelog:major' label and a '## What this means for you' section (see CONTRIBUTING.md). Entries are newest first. An entry may carry a validated optional 'media' object; reproduce assets with tools/capture_changelog_media.py. Do not hand-edit the 'text' field without also updating changelog.html's auto-generated block (run: node tools/gen_changelog.mjs --rebuild).

entries.text: If you're watching a notice from an agency that files its awards somewhere other than the City Record, you can now ask to be emailed the moment that award shows up. Click "Email me when the award registers" on the notice, confirm your email once, and you're done — no extra emails until there's real news, and it's the exact same possible-vs-confirmed distinction the notice page already shows you.

before.alt: A notice page before the award watch button was added.

after.alt: The same notice page with the Email me when the award registers button.

before.alt: A notice page before the award watch button was added.

after.alt: The same notice page with the Email me when the award registers button.

recording.caption: Find a covered notice, choose the award watch, enter reader@example.com, and reach the email confirmation step.

entries.text: Every note that says your answer lives in another public system now hands you the door: click through to the exact record, the filtered dataset view, or an explanation of what was checked. If a source can't be linked yet, the site says so honestly instead of showing a dead mention.
Click a link in a CROL-List digest email. You used to land on a bare notice page. Now you see why it matched. The matching word is highlighted. You also see a plain summary of your watch.

Has a saved alert gone quiet? If it finds nothing new for a couple of months, its next email says so. That email links back to the alerts page. Your exact search is already loaded there. You can broaden it in one step.

Some agencies do not file awards in the City Record. They post them elsewhere. The site now checks. It looks at outside sources. It shows what it finds. It links to the source. If nothing turns up, it says so.
Opening a notice with no confirmed prior-award history now offers a "Show possible earlier rounds" option — a set of caveated maybes for your own judgment, instead of a dead end.

The stats page's "all time" numbers, topic/lens breakdowns, and day-by-day history now say exactly how far back they can honestly account for — no more numbers that quietly undercount, and no more "since launch" where that isn't true. Digests, searches, and now active watches are also easier to read at a glance, with the technical plumbing (feed fetches, API checks) moved to its own clearly-labeled section at the bottom, translated across all ten shipping languages.

Typing a full sentence like "education contracts over $200K due in 3 months" into the Alerts quiz's keyword field now previews real matching results and saves an alert with the actual interpreted filters — the same thing you'd get from the "Ask" box, and the same thing that gets saved when you subscribe. A single word or a quoted phrase still searches literally as before.
Alert emails and the "Preview my digest" tool now show exactly which word matched and where — no more guessing why an unrelated-looking notice showed up in your search results.

Opening the Land or Staffing tab for the first time now shows a real, current example — a live rezoning application, or the city's most common job title with its salary band and career ladder — instead of an empty panel waiting for you to search. Your own searches and shared links work exactly as before.
The stats page now shows day-by-day history of digests sent and searches asked, with recovered and live-counted days honestly labeled.
You can now build an alert that watches one agency, or only awards, or only open solicitations, not just an amount or a deadline.
Typing something like "education contracts over $200K due in 3 months" into an alert now matches the category, amount, and deadline together, not just one of them.
Notice pages now show whether a recurring bid was awarded before, and to whom, not just its own paper trail.
The stats page now uses plain-language labels, shows all-time totals, and breaks down digest matches by topic.
The Money tab is now called Contracts, and a notice page now leads with that notice's own facts before agency-wide totals.
The homepage now says how many notices came in today, digest email titles are clickable, and a new tagline explains what the site does.
You can now read every page in Arabic and Urdu, right to left.
Added Bengali, Haitian Creole, Korean, French, and Polish.
Added Chinese (simplified) and Russian.
Closed the last known accessibility gaps: every control now has a visible focus ring, an accurate label, and enough color contrast to read.
Every page, not just the homepage, now translates into Español, and your language choice is remembered across visits.
